text: In this video, we present a robotic sleeve gastrectomy and an encounter with a very large liver and show how to potentially handle dealing with a very large wet lobe of the liver.
The surgeon measures six centimeters proximal to the pylorus and marks this area with a suture.

The surgeon enters the lesser sac and begins taking down the greater omentum along the greater curvature of the stomach, proceeding towards the pylorus. The duodenum is visible posterior to the stomach. The dissection continues along the greater curvature towards the short gastric vessels and the spleen.
The surgeon visualizes the bougie inside the patient's stomach, which is used as a guide during the gastrectomy procedure.
The surgeon adjusts the position of the large left lobe of the liver to bring the anatomical structures for dissection closer into view, facilitating the continuation of the gastrectomy procedure.
The surgeon assesses the surgical field and determines that visualization is inadequate, prompting plans for further mobilization of the large left lobe of the liver to improve access.
A small capsular tear is visible, which is controlled by applying pressure to the affected area.
A suture is placed to retract and elevate the proximal part of the left lobe of the liver, creating a canopy-like effect to improve visualization.
The surgeon places a piece of gauze over the fatty liver to distribute force evenly on the capsule, aiming to prevent tearing during manipulation.

The surgeon places one end of the suture on the Nathanson retractor and threads the other end through the falciform ligament, facilitating elevation and stabilization of the liver for improved visualization.
Using the robotic platform, the surgeon manipulates and places sutures on the interior aspect of the abdomen, demonstrating precise control and positioning of the sutures.
The surgeon visualizes the diaphragm and continues the dissection towards the left crura, carefully navigating the anatomical structures in the upper abdomen.
The surgeon visualizes the spleen, indicating correct orientation, while continuing dissection from the posterior aspect.
It's very important to keep a good visualization in your mind of where the left gastric vasculature would be located.
The surgeon carefully dissects the filmy tissue along the lesser curvature of the stomach, taking care to avoid injury to the visible blood vessels in this area.
The surgeon has completely mobilized the cardiac region, with visible separation from surrounding structures.
The surgeon identifies the left crura, visible near the diaphragm, as the dissection progresses towards this anatomical landmark.
The surgeon carefully removes the previously placed suture, ensuring clear visualization of the surrounding tissue and structures.
The surgeon carefully observes the vessels along the lesser curvature of the stomach, noting the excellent pulsation of the blood vessels in this area.
The liver is retracted using robotic arm number three, with gauze placed to prevent injury to the liver capsule during the retraction process.
The surgeon prepares the stapler with a black load for the first firing, ensuring that the device does not impinge on the gastric incisor during the gastrectomy.
The surgeon attempts to visualize both the anterior and posterior elbows of the stomach, preparing for the imprecation of the staple line.
A large lipoma is discovered along the gastroesophageal junction. The surgeon decides to partially remove the lipoma to improve visualization of the angle of His.
Of course, great care needs to be taken with a step, not to accidentally burn the underlying stomach
Also, you have to be careful to make sure that the redundant portion of the posterior frontis and cardia are out of your sleeve and remain with the specimen component.
The surgeon moves the specimen completely out of the surgical field and begins to imprecate the staple line on the stomach.
The surgeon uses the Lambert technique to imprecate the staple line, avoiding direct suturing through the staple line.
While it may take a little bit longer to over-sew the staple line, one potentially saves on the cost of utilizing the buttressing material.
And while there's no definitive answer as to the best way to reinforce the staple line, this is just one of the few good options that are available.
The methylene glue test was performed and was negative.
The surgical field shows no active bleeding from the previous capsular tear, and biological fibrin glue is applied to the area.
The surgeon applies additional staples to the area with increased oozing to control bleeding and reinforce the staple line.
Thank you for your attention.
